data.title: Net Power plunges after reporting quarterly cash burn
data.description:
Net Power’s (NYSE:NPWR) stock on Monday fell as much 66% after the green-energy company reported quarterly results.

It ended 2024 with $533 million in cash, cash equivalents and investments, down from $580 million in the prior quarter, mostly because of $13 million in operating cash outflows and $29 million in capital expenditures.

Net Power’s (NYSE:NPWR) annual loss narrowed to $49.2 million in 2024 from $77.2 million a year earlier.

“2024 was a year of significant achievement amidst challenging market conditions for commercializing new technologies,” Danny Rice, chief executive of Net Power (NPWR), said in a statement.

data.title: Robinhood fined $30M in Finra settlement over AML, trading violations; stock drops
data.description:
Robinhood Markets (NASDAQ:HOOD) stock dropped 13% in Monday morning trading as the retail trading app agreed to pay a $29.8M fine ordered by the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority to resolve several alleged violations tied to its clearing technology system and anti-money laundering programs.

Finra accused Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD) of giving customers inaccurate or incomplete disclosures associated with the brokerage's practice of so-called "collaring" market order by converting them to limit orders.

The regulator also found that the trading platform failed to create and implement "reasonable anti-money laundering programs, which caused the firms to fail to detect, investigate or report suspicious activity, including manipulative trading, suspicious money movements and instances where customers’ accounts were taken over by third-party hackers."

What's more, HOOD allegedly failed to properly supervise its clearing technology system, which was used to clear trades for Robinhood Financial, including responding to several signs of processing delays amid growing trading volume on the platform.

Without admitting or denying any wrongdoing, Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD) agreed to pay $26M for the violations, with an additional $3.75M in restitution to its customers.

The order, released on Friday, also noted that HOOD lacked in the supervision of social media communications promoting the company that were posted by compensated social media influencers. "Some of these communications included statements that were promissory or not fair and balanced, and thus misleading to investors."

Conversely, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission last month closed its investigation into Robinhood's (HOOD) crypto business without taking any enforcement action.

data.title: Has the government forgotten what a real recession is?
data.description:
The U.S. government may have forgotten what a “real” recession is like, said Dario Perkins, managing director of global macro at GlobalData TS Lombard.

The latest real U.S. recession happened 17 years ago at the start of the global economic downturn, “and the fake Covid-19 downturn may have given them an inflated sense of omnipotence,” with the country rebounding rapidly from it and returning to normal employment levels without long-term issues in a relatively short period.

But as President Donald Trump said the U.S. is in “a period of transition,” not ruling out a recession while Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency and the administration are doing something “very big” for the economy and its future, “it is odd to see U.S. policymakers talk as if they want to inflict damage on the economy, or at least do things that risk causing damage,” said Perkins.

He cautioned that the combination of tariffs and DOGE’s cuts is dangerous.

“Markets are accustomed to Whatever-it-Takes policy,” he said, but what is currently happening to the economy is new. “It is Muck-Around-and-Find-Out policy.”

DOGE cuts could generate unemployment at a time when hiring is weak. “This could spill over into a broader loss of confidence, triggering further job losses,” said Perkins.

“We know that small rises in unemployment usually precede much bigger rises, and the Fed may not react quickly,” he said. “It has tariffs to worry about.”

More on U.S. economy:

  • Point72’s Cohen is ‘pretty negative’ on US economy amid tariffs, immigration, DOGE
  • US economy grows slightly in recent weeks: Fed's Beige Book report
  • US economy on path to 2% inflation, but policy should stay where it is, Atlanta Fed's Bostic says
  • US to launch probe into impact of Chinese chips, policies on American economy
  • Nasdaq drops 2%, S&P, and Dow fall as well as concerns about the economy grow

data.title: Nasdaq plunges 3%, S&P, and Dow sink as well as Wall Street sell-off continues
data.description:
Investors and traders on Wall Street have watched the broader market averages plunge on Monday with the Nasdaq Composite is leading the way lower as tech sells-off. Concerns around the health of the economy continue to cast a darker cloud on equities. 

Falling the hardest has been the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) which has dropped 3% so far on Monday. Also deep in the red is the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) which is lower by 1.7% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) which has come down by 0.7%.

From a sector-by-sector stance, market participants observed that seven of the 11 S&P sectors trade lower with Info Tech and Communication Services suffering the most. At the other end of the spectrum Energy leads all other segments higher even as oil (CL1:COM) trades lower by 0.5%.

In the Treasury market, yields have declined as investors purchase bonds. The shorter end U.S. 2-Year Treasury yield (US2Y) fell by 7 basis points to 4.00%. Meanwhile, the longer end U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield (US10Y) dipped 7 basis points to 4.20%.

The fears of a potential recession in the U.S. grew after Trump was asked if he was expecting a recession this year in the interview. “I hate to predict things like that,” he said. “There is a period of transition because what we’re doing is very big. We’re bringing wealth back to America. That’s a big thing.”

"Trump also failed to rule out a recession. After the 2024 soft landing, markets are getting more concerned about economic growth risks. As the pandemic illustrated, supply chain disruption can create growth problems. Disruption to trade and government both raise risks," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Furthermore, adding to the bearish sentiment, China’s return to deflation has fueled worries about global growth prospects.

China's consumer prices fell 0.7% year-on-year in February, a 13-month low, exceeding the expected 0.5% drop and reversing January’s 0.5% rise.

The economic calendar is light on Monday, with no real announcements being made. Investors will however receive the latest inflation data on Wednesday as markets get set to receive CPI data. 

As for stocks that are on the move, Moderna (MRNA) has climbed 5.1% despite Wall Stret’s sell-off, while shares of MicroStrategy (MSTR) have dropped by 12.5%.

data.title: Karman Holdings rated Buy in new coverage at Citi
data.description:
Karman Holdings (NYSE:KRMN) on Monday was rated Buy in new coverage by analysts at financial-services firm Citi. They said the maker of spacecraft parts including what are known as fairings is uniquely positioned between major defense contractors and traditional suppliers.

“The company is focused on three of the fastest growing areas of US defense spending, including nuclear deterrence, tactical missiles and space,” Jason Gursky, analyst at Citi, said in a March 10 report. “We expect the company to generate roughly 20% revenue growth and 300 basis points of margin expansion through 2027.”

While the Trump administration’s Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE, is seeking to root out wasteful federal spending, Karman’s (NYSE:KRMN) is likely shielded from cuts because of its role in nuclear deterrence, tactical missiles and space, according to Citi.

Karman (KRMN) last month went public in an offering that raised $506 million for itself and existing shareholders, hitting a valuation of about $3 billion.

Citi set a price target of $42 a share on Karman’s (KRMN) shares, based on an estimated enterprise value that’s 30 times earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization for 2027.

Growing spending on defense spending worldwide is supportive of Karman’s (KRMN) business, Citi said.

“Defense budgets in the U.S. and Europe are likely headed higher in the coming years, particularly as the geopolitical environment is quickly evolving toward what appears to be a multi-polar world,” according to Citi.

data.title: Leviathan Gold announces non-brokered private placement of units
data.description:
• Leviathan Gold (TSXV:LVX:CA) has announced a non-brokered private placement of up to 35M units in the capital of the Co. at a price of $0.10 per Unit for aggregate gross proceeds of up to $3.5M.
  • Each Unit shall be comprised of one common share and one-half of one Common Share purchase warrant of the Company.
  • Each Warrant shall entitle the holder thereof to acquire one Common Share in the capital of the Co. at a price of $0.15 per Common Share for a period of 24 months from the date of issuance.
  • Proceeds from this Offering will be used primarily for exploration in Australia and Bosnia and for general working capital purposes.

data.title: Tesla falls 8% as bearish sentiment continues to build
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) opened with a 7.9% swing lower on Monday amid broad selling in the stock market to trade at its lowest level of 2025. The EV stock is down over 40% for 2025 amid the near-term concerns, and trades below its 200-day moving average.

Elon Musk's association with President Trump's administration and his role in the Department of Government Efficiency has led to protests over the last week. Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) locations in New York City, Chicago, Austin, Portland, Seattle, Boston, Orlando, West Palm Beach, Denver, and Tulsa were some of the sites of the organized protests by consumers. Outside the U.S. protests in Lisbon, Barcelona, and London attracted large crowds.

On Wall Street, UBS lowered its deliveries forecast on Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and cut its price target to $225 from $259. "While the long-term story at TSLA has shifted to AI (robo-taxis and humanoid robots) and progress there continues, we believe these are longer dated opportunities that the premium multiple already (more than) considers," wrote analyst Joseph Spak.

Last week, Wedbush analyst Dan Ives predicted that investors will eventually look past DOGE-related issues. Wedbush Securities added Tesla (TSLA) to its Best Ideas List amid what it calls a gut-check moment for the electric vehicle stock, with bears seen as controlling the narrative.

Analyst Dan Ives said Tesla (TSLA) is at the start of the biggest innovation and technology cycle in its history ahead over the next few years. The firm expects a lower cost, sub-$35K new model before this summer that will help drive pent-up EV consumer demand globally and get Tesla back on the growth track. In addition, the launch of unsupervised FSD in Austin this June is anticipated to kick off the autonomous era at Tesla (TSLA) that Wedbush values at $1 trillion alone on a sum-of-the-parts valuation.

On Seeking Alpha, opinion is sharply divided. Stock Waves posted a new Strong Buy rating on TSLA based on technical analysis, while Cash Flow Venue and Simple Investment Ideas both have recent articles backing Strong Sell ratings.

EV watch: Notably, Lucid Group (LCID) was up 2.2% in early trading on Monday to bounce off its 2025 low.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Monday: Crypto stocks, TSLA, RKT, CKPT, and more
data.description:
Stock futures dipped on Monday, with the Dow down 300 points and the Nasdaq sliding nearly 490 points, as investors grappled with uncertainty surrounding President Trump’s tariff policy and looked ahead to key economic data releases. Meanwhile, Bitcoin declined after an executive order outlining plans for a strategic U.S. Bitcoin reserve failed to reassure markets.

Here are some of Monday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Checkpoint Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CKPT) +66% – Shares surged after India’s Sun Pharma announced a $355M acquisition, offering $4.10 per share in cash, a 66% premium to CKPT’s last close. Shareholders will also receive up to $0.70 per share in contingent value rights based on milestone achievements. The deal, expected to close in Q2 2025, strengthens Sun Pharma’s global onco-dermatology portfolio with CKPT’s FDA-approved treatment, UNLOXCYT (cosibelimab-ipdl).
  • DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) +6% – Shares climbed after S&P Dow Jones Indices announced the food delivery giant will join the S&P 500, replacing BorgWarner (NYSE:BWA) before trading begins on March 24.

Biggest stock losers

  • Bitcoin dropped to the $81K level, pressuring crypto stocks— Bitcoin extended its decline, falling 5.3% to $81,286, marking its fifth straight session in the red after President Trump’s Bitcoin reserve plan failed to boost sentiment. Crypto-related equities took a hit, with MicroStrategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) -7.2%, Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN) -6.4%, Riot Platforms (NASDAQ:RIOT) -5.1%, MARA Holdings (NASDAQ:MARA) -6.7%, Bit Digital (NASDAQ:BTBT) -4.3%, CleanSpark (NASDAQ:CLSK) -5.2%, Hut 8 (NASDAQ:HUT) -5.6%, and Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD) -6.5% trading lower.
  • Rocket Companies (RKT) -11% – Shares plunged after announcing a $1.75B acquisition of Redfin (NASDAQ:RDFN), merging one of the most-visited brokerage platforms with the largest mortgage lender. The all-stock transaction values Redfin at $12.50 per share, a ~63% premium to its 30-day volume-weighted average price. Under the agreement, each Redfin share will convert into 0.7926 shares of Rocket’s Class A common stock. The deal, expected to close in Q2 or Q3 2025, is projected to boost Rocket’s adjusted EPS by late 2026 and generate over $200M in run-rate synergies by 2027. Following the announcement, Redfin shares soared 81%.
  • Tesla (TSLA) -8% - Shares dropped as the automaker faced protests and vandalism at its facilities in the U.S. and Europe. The backlash comes amid rising public discontent over CEO Elon Musk’s perceived involvement in the Donald Trump administration.

data.title: Palantir partners with Voyager on AI solutions to avoid space collisions
data.description:
Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) entered the final frontier and partnered with Voyager Technologies to develop artificial intelligence-powered solutions to mitigate collisions and threats for objects orbiting Earth.

Shares fell 6% in early Monday trading.

Voyager, a defense technology company, is working with Palantir to develop enhanced Space Domain Awareness, which pulls data from existing on-orbit assets using Voyager's VPX signal processing electronics and chassis, signals intelligence software and mission services capabilities and Palantir's AI/machine-learning engines.

"We are further extending the reach of our AI solutions from ground to space, a critical frontier where demands are intensifying as the environment grows increasingly contested," said Robert Imig, head of USG Research and Development at Palantir.

"We're developing an integrated solution, harnessing the power of AI with Palantir's software stack and taking advantage of real-time radio frequency streams and imagery to perform space-based object detection, identification, classification and tracking," added Matt Magaña, president of Defense and National Security at Voyager.

Voyager is a Denver-based company that recently rebranded itself to become a more defense-focused firm. This included several new executive appointments, such as naming Magaña, a former Raytheon (RTX) executive to his current role. Wallis Laughrey, who formerly worked at Raytheon and Anduril Industries, joined as chief strategy officer. Dan Caine, a retired U.S. Air Force lieutenant general and former associate director of the CIA, was appointed chairman of Voyager's Defense and National Security Advisory Board earlier this year.
